expectations.text: States that GDPR compliance provides a strong foundation (privacy notices, rights processes, processor agreements, data minimization) but does NOT automatically satisfy CCPA/CPRA
expectations.evidence: Response acknowledges strong overlap (privacy notices, rights workflows, DPAs, data minimisation) while being explicit that GDPR compliance does not automatically satisfy CCPA/CPRA.

expectations.text: Identifies CCPA/CPRA-specific additions needed: 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link + GPC signal compliance, and 'Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information' link (CPRA)
expectations.evidence: Response covers the 'Do Not Sell or Share' link, GPC signal compliance with technical implementation requirement, and the 'Limit Use of SPI' link for CPRA sensitive personal information categories.

expectations.text: Explains the opt-out vs. opt-in model difference: CCPA/CPRA is primarily opt-out (consumers must request opt-out) whereas GDPR requires opt-in consent for most sensitive processing
expectations.evidence: Response includes a comparison table showing how the same scenarios are handled differently under each regime, explaining CCPA/CPRA's opt-out architecture vs. GDPR's consent-gate opt-in model.

expectations.text: Notes that CCPA/CPRA has no lawful basis requirement for general PI processing — businesses can collect PI without one of GDPR's six legal bases
expectations.evidence: Response explains that GDPR's Article 6 six bases have no equivalent under CCPA/CPRA, and that legitimate interests assessments are irrelevant in the California context.

expectations.text: Highlights the CCPA/CPRA private right of action for data breaches ($100–$750 per consumer per incident) which has no GDPR equivalent, and the need to verify vendor contracts meet CCPA service provider requirements
expectations.evidence: Response details $100–$750 per consumer per incident statutory damages exposure, contrasts it with GDPR's regulatory-fine model, and provides side-by-side table of DPA vs. CCPA service provider contract clause differences.
